sections.text: Any visitor can suggest a change to this page. An AI moderates the suggestion, a second AI generates the edit, and the result becomes a real GitHub pull request that CI checks, auto-merges, and Vercel deploys — all within 30–90 seconds. No human in the loop.

stats.label: changes have a real GitHub PR

steps.description: Rate-limited by IP and saved as queued — no AI called yet
steps.title: Receive

steps.description: Kill switch, scope classification, and moderation — three sequential Haiku checks before any Sonnet spend
steps.title: Gate

steps.description: Sonnet reads current page content and your suggestion, then calls update_sections or update_theme
steps.title: Generate

steps.description: Output validated against Zod schemas, then written to a new branch and a pull request opened
steps.title: Commit

steps.description: CI verifies only allowlisted files changed, PR auto-merges, Vercel rebuilds, live push to all open tabs
steps.title: Ship

sections.body: A GitHub Actions workflow blocks any pull request that touches files outside a strict allowlist (content/, theme/). This check runs on GitHub's infrastructure, independent of the agent pipeline — the CI wall is enforced at the repository level, not inside the AI models.
sections.title: The agent cannot break the site

items: Zod schema validation — bad shapes, missing fields, and out-of-range values are caught before any commit
Element locking — further submissions for the same element return HTTP 409 while a suggestion is in-flight
Kill switch — a single setting halts the pipeline before any AI call; no redeploy needed
Anthropic spend cap — a hard monthly limit; when reached, the pipeline marks suggestions failed
Held-comment queue — low-confidence outputs are held for owner review before re-generating against current state

sections.body: The agent can rewrite text, split or merge sections, reorder the page, add new typed sections, or hide existing ones. It cannot write arbitrary HTML, invent new section types, or touch application code. Output is validated against strict Zod schemas before any commit.
sections.title: What the agent can and cannot do

items.description: App framework — pages, API routes, and ISR cache
items.name: Next.js

items.description: Claude Haiku moderates; Claude Sonnet generates the patch
items.name: Anthropic

items.description: Durable multi-step pipeline with retries and a hold queue
items.name: Inngest

items.description: Stores suggestions, settings, and streams real-time status
items.name: Supabase

items.description: CI allowlist check and deploy webhook on every merge
items.name: GitHub Actions

items.description: Hosts the site and rebuilds on every merged PR
items.name: Vercel

sections.text: Hover any element on this page — a small icon appears in the corner. Click it, type a suggestion in plain English, and submit. The pipeline runs in the background; the page updates itself when the deploy lands.

items: "Rewrite the opening paragraph for a non-technical audience"
"Add a section about what happens when a suggestion is rejected"
"Merge the two safety sections into one"
"Change the 3D shape from a Dodecahedron to a TorusKnot"
"Add a second orbiting object to the 3D scene"

sections.text: Every agent-opened pull request follows a consistent commit format so agent changes are easy to spot in the git history:

sections.code: agent(elevator-pitch): rewrite opening paragraph to active voice

sections.text: Every change has a real GitHub PR, real CI, and a real deploy — because real work is happening at each step.
The thing is interesting because it's real, not because it claims to be.
Every change has a real pull request →
